vulnerability: Citrix ADC Gateway Remote Code Execution
description: Critical RCE vulnerability in Citrix ADC (NetScaler) Gateway discovered by internal vulnerability scan. Citrix has acknowledged the vulnerability and assigned CTX-2025-0089. Citrix states the fix requires a major version upgrade (ADC 14.1 build 30.x) and cannot be delivered as a hotfix. The major version upgrade requires application compatibility testing, SSL certificate re-import, and load balancer rule migration. Citrix has not provided a firm release date — initially estimated Q4 2025, then pushed to 'early 2026.'
vendor_status: Vendor acknowledged. Fix requires major version upgrade. No hotfix available. Release date pushed from Q4 2025 to 'early 2026' with no firm date.
compensating_controls: 1. WAF (Imperva) with virtual patching rules deployed for known exploit patterns. 2. Citrix ADC management interface restricted to dedicated management VLAN (10.30.100.0/24) via firewall ACL. 3. Enhanced monitoring — CrowdStrike Falcon EDR on ADC with behavioral detection enabled. 4. IDS/IPS signatures for exploit patterns deployed on perimeter firewall. 5. Rate limiting on Gateway authentication endpoint to prevent brute-force attack chaining.
interim_mitigations_applied: Citrix recommended workaround (disable DTLS protocol) applied September 1. However, Citrix advisory CTX-2025-0089 rev 3 (November 15) states workaround 'only partially mitigates the vulnerability — attack vector through standard TLS remains available.' Partial mitigation only.
monthly_reviews: Review 1 (Sep 25): All controls verified. Citrix says Q4 fix. Review 2 (Oct 25): Citrix pushed timeline to 'early 2026.' No firm date. Controls verified. Review 3 (Nov 25): Citrix advisory rev 3 states workaround only partial. Requested updated timeline — Citrix could not confirm. All compensating controls verified. Review 4 (Dec 28): No update from Citrix on release date. Compensating controls verified. 130 days since first detection. 4 months of monthly reviews with no vendor fix date confirmed.
review_notes: Judgment question: Citrix has not released a fix for 130+ days and the workaround is confirmed only partial. The organization has followed the exception process correctly (CISO approval, compensating controls, monthly reviews). However: (1) Citrix keeps pushing the timeline with no firm date, (2) the workaround is only partial, (3) the vulnerability is Critical (CVSS 9.4), and (4) compensating controls cannot fully mitigate an RCE on a gateway device. At what point should the organization consider replacing the product rather than waiting indefinitely? Is 'waiting for the vendor' a valid indefinite strategy for a CVSS 9.4 vulnerability?

description: HP LaserJet firmware vulnerability. HP acknowledged in HP Security Bulletin HPSBPI-2025-0012. Fix expected in firmware v5.8.0.2 (release: November 2025). Patch was released on November 18.
vendor_status: HP released firmware v5.8.0.2 on November 18. Patch available.
compensating_controls: 1. Printer isolated on dedicated print VLAN. 2. Admin interface restricted to 2 named IPs. 3. Firmware auto-update disabled to prevent unauthorized changes.
interim_mitigations_applied: HP workaround (disable JetDirect features) applied July 15.
monthly_reviews: Review 1 (Aug 15): HP says November fix. Controls verified. Review 2 (Sep 15): No change. Review 3 (Oct 15): HP confirms November 18 release. Review 4 (Nov 15): HP patch released Nov 18 — scheduled for December MW-PROD. Review 5 (Dec 15): PATCH NOT APPLIED. December maintenance window used for OS patching. Rescheduled to January. Patch has been available for 27 days but not applied.
review_notes: HP released the fix on November 18. The vendor dependency is resolved. However, the patch has not been applied — it has been available for 41 days (as of Dec 29) but was not prioritized in the December patch window. The exception was filed because the vendor had not released a fix. Now the vendor HAS released a fix and the exception should be closed by applying the patch. Instead, the patch is deferred to January. This is no longer a vendor dependency — it is a patching prioritization failure.
